quotes.verbatim_quote:
In the key of B-Flat Major. seventh chords formed on tones I and IV are Major Seventh chords. In
the same key, seventh chords formed on tones II, II] and VI are Minor Seventh chords. Seventh chords
formed on tones V and VII are Dominant and Half-Diminished, respectively.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage establishes the foundational rule for which chord quality belongs to each scale degree in any major key — the bedrock of Roman-numeral harmonic analysis used throughout the chapter.

quotes.topic: most common m7 and M7 functions
quotes.verbatim_quote:
We can state in general terms that, in most instances, the M7 chord is likely to function as IM7
rather than FVM7. Also the m7 chord functions more commonly as a IIm7 than as a HIm7 or Vim?.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This practical heuristic tells the improviser which function to assume by default when a chord's key is ambiguous, reducing the decision burden in real-time playing.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The Hm7-V7-IM7 progression is important, therefore, because it establishes a specific key. Also, the
scales of these three chords will share exactly the same key signature. In fig. 25, the Gm7 uses a
Dorian Mode on the note G (which has the parent key of F Major); and the FM6 uses the F Major
scale.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This explicitly states the theoretical reason the IIm7–V7–IM7 matters for improvisation: shared key signature means a single parent scale covers all three chords, and it names the Dorian mode as the correct choice for the IIm7.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Figure 27 is a chart which will help the student recognize the IIm7-V7 and Ilm7-V7-IM7 progres-
sions as they are used in standard tunes. It is advisable to MEMORIZE the chart.
quotes.why_load_bearing: The explicit instruction to memorize the all-keys chart is a pedagogical directive that signals this reference table is a prerequisite for all subsequent pattern work in the chapter.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
A musician who can read accurately in gronps of notes, rather than just a few at a time, will develop
good reading habits and greatly improve sight reading. In the same manner, an improviser who can
read groups of chords that are related to each other, rather than improvising on one chord at a time,
will also be developing good reading habits which consequently allow for more spontaneity in
playing.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage articulates the pedagogical principle behind the entire chapter's pattern approach: thinking in harmonic units (IIm7–V7) rather than individual chords is the skill that enables idiomatic jazz improvisation.

quotes.topic: Pattern 123: ascending m7 scale over IIm7–V7
quotes.verbatim_quote: This pattern uses the scale of the m7 chord in one complete octave, in the ascending form.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the defining rule for Pattern 123 — it specifies exactly which scale source and which direction govern the first one-measure IIm7–V7 exercise.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This pattern is based on the m7th scale. Arabic numbers indicate the succession of tones, from the m7 scale, necessary to complete this pattern with remaining IIm7-V7 (one measure) progressions in all keys.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This instruction tells the student how to transpose Pattern 124 through all keys using scale-degree numbers, making the Arabic-numeral notation the transposition mechanism rather than interval counting.
